text: The surgeon demonstrates the port placement for a hand-assisted nephrectomy on the right side, with the patient positioned in a full-flank position using a beanbag. A seven-centimeter muscle-splitting incision is made for the hand port, and two additional ten-millimeter ports are placed, including one at the umbilicus. A three-millimeter alligator grasper is used to hold the liver up as a self-retaining retractor. The surgeon's hand is inserted through the hand port, creating a laparoscopic insufflation seal. The gallbladder, liver, and kidney are clearly visible in the thin patient.

The surgeon begins the nephrectomy by formally taking down the line of Toldt, using the hand as a retractor to aid in mobilization. The hand-assisted approach enhances retraction capabilities compared to purely laparoscopic techniques.

The surgeon uses a harmonic scalpel to take down the line of Toldt, extending the dissection towards the liver and diaphragm. The hand is positioned over the kidney to sweep the Gerota's fascia downwards, revealing the kidney in full view. The peritoneum is safely dissected along the liver edge up to the incision near the diaphragm.

The surgeon uses their hand as a retractor to mobilize the colon downwards, revealing the duodenum beneath. Two fingers are used to retract the colon, while the pinky finger pushes the kidney laterally and the thumb pushes the bowel medially, creating retraction points for further dissection.

The surgeon carefully navigates the surgical plane to ensure the duodenum is moved aside, revealing the renal vein. With the duodenum and bowel repositioned, the surgeon proceeds to free the lower pole of the kidney and completes the mobilization of the colon, extending the dissection down to the pelvis.

The surgeon uses their hand to hold the kidney upright, revealing the vena cava and an aberrant crossing vessel. The lower pole of the kidney is elevated while the colon is being mobilized. Another small crossing vessel is visible near the kidney, possibly an aberrant gonadal vessel.

The surgeon positions the kidney upright using the thumb and forefinger, exposing the psoas muscle. Several aberrant vessels, including a small crossing artery and vein, are visible. The duodenum is positioned downward, revealing a clear plane of fascia beneath which the main renal vein is expected to be seen.

The surgeon follows a predictable fascial plane beneath the plane used to mobilize the duodenum, moving across the top of the kidney. Gyrotis fat is released to spare the adrenal gland, revealing the branching renal vein. The surgeon aims to visualize the kidney parenchyma, ensuring no vascular structures remain. Windows are created in the Gyrotis fat, which is stapled to manage potential bleeding, especially near the vena cava.

The surgeon uses both hands to dissect, employing either a sucker or hand as a retractor. The upper pole fat is finished, sparing the adrenal gland. The kidney is held straight up, revealing the renal vein. Fingers are placed behind the hilum to dissect out visible vessels.

The surgeon uses their thumb as a retractor to hold the kidney straight up while working on a crossing vessel inferiorly. The middle finger is positioned around the hilum, pushing on the artery to expose it from underneath the vein, allowing for dissection of the vessels. The surgeon proceeds retroperitoneally along the psoas muscle to fully mobilize the kidney, enabling it to be held upright.

The surgeon identifies the psoas muscle and notes the hilum is not yet finished, ensuring readiness to address any bleeding. The lateral attachments are being released, and the ureter is located for the first time. Visible are aberrant gonadal vessels and crossing arteries and veins, which are noted but not prioritized in the current step.

The surgeon's finger is passed underneath the renal artery, pulling it out from beneath the renal vein. The artery is gradually exposed as the finger creates a window for the stapler. The kidney is held upright with upward traction, ensuring the vessels are under tension for safe manipulation. The surgeon confirms the window is clear for the stapler, with no obstruction from surrounding structures.

The surgeon uses a harmonic scalpel to address lymphatics while preparing the kidney for staple firing. Several aberrant arteries and veins are visible, and the ureter is now in view. Small metal clips are applied to secure the small aberrant vessels adjacent to the ureter.

The surgeon identifies the main renal artery and prepares to use an automatic firing stapler to ligate it. An aberrant crossing artery is also visible, which the surgeon plans to take next. The veins are left behind, and the surgeon avoids getting too close to the vena cava while ensuring the vein is taken before it branches.

The surgeon isolates and prepares to take the remaining vein, leaving the ureter as the final structure to be addressed. Metal sutures are typically used for the ureter, with consideration for using a wet clip if the ureter is hydronephrotic or if reflux is suspected.

The surgeon presents the kidney specimen, noting the absence of visible tumor due to its centralized location. The specimen is pulled straight out of the abdomen with minimal bleeding observed. A sponge is applied to address any bleeding, and the surgical site is inspected with insufflation pressure reduced to five to check for oozing or bleeding.

The surgeon shows the staple line on the renal vein, with the vena cava and accessory artery visible alongside the stump of the artery. No leakage is observed, indicating satisfactory hemostasis. The bivalve kidney specimen is displayed, revealing the centrally located tumor that necessitated a heminephrectomy.
